title: Definition of Shellfish: Inclusion of Cephalopods

contexts: mmary of the proposed rule, please see the proposed rule summary document in Docket No. FWS-HQ-;LE-2026-0628 on https://www.regulations.gov . SUPPLEMENTARY INFORMATION: Public Comments We are opening a 30

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: I oppose the proposed rule. I grew up along the Puget Sound, and currently live in the San Juan Islands in Washington state. In my community, commercial fishing is central to the local economy, and the sustainable management of marine species is a lived reality. Regulatory frameworks governing how marine wildlife enters and moves through commerce have tangib...

FWS-HQ-LE-2026-0628-0031.title: Comment from Mid-Atlantic Fishery Management Council

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: The proposed revision to the definition of “shellfish” in 50 CFR 10.12 would replace the current morphological formulation (“having a shell”) with a taxonomic definition that expressly includes Mollusca (including cephalopods) and Order Decapoda. The stated purpose is to eliminate ambiguity regarding the treatment of cephalopods and to improve consistency in...

I oppose the proposed rule. The definition of shellfish in federal regulations plays an important role in determining which wildlife products qualify for regulatory exemptions. Expanding that definition to include cephalopods could extend the shellfish exemption under 50 CFR §14.21 to species that were not previously covered. Because import/export licensing ...

FWS-HQ-LE-2026-0628 This Change is unnecessary ! It is Ridiculous You claim it will clear things up. Hogwash, This is Arbitrary and Capricious! Shellfish Have a SHELL! There will be a hardship caused by this- Lots of Money and time will be wasted trying to rewrite the rules.

I oppose the proposed rule. The Service describes the proposed rule as deregulatory and intended to reduce ambiguity. However, redefining shellfish to include cephalopods appears to expand the licensing exemption found in 50 CFR §14.21. Because shellfish imported for human or animal consumption are exempt from the import/export licensing requirement, the pro...

I have concerns about this proposed rule. The U.S. Fish and Wildlife Service says the definition has led to inconsistent treatment and an unnecessary regulatory burden. Where? To who? Only vague references to “inconsistent treatment” and “unnecessary regulatory burden” appear in the rule proposal. I am concerned by the lack of any apparent empirical evidence...

As an individual who, in my professional capacity, works closely with the U.S. squid fishing industry to ensure that the fishery operates in a robust and sustainable manner, I fully support the USFWS proposed rule to revise the definition of shellfish to clearly include squid, octopus, and cuttlefish. This proposed change is long overdue, but very welcome. T...

This is a class assignment in which I am learning how to use Regulations.gov. I chose this rule to comment on because of my particular interest in protecting marine wildlife. I do not support this proposed regulation. Grouping cephalopods with shellfish could have detrimental effects on monitoring data and scientific integrity. First, by grouping the two tog...

Hello, I am currently a law student, and while I am not in the commercial fishing industry, I would like to express my concern with this proposed regulation. The lay definition of shellfish is simple, fish with a shell. This is a clear definition, because an average person can look at an aquatic invertebrate and know whether it is a shellfish or not. This pr...

I oppose the proposed rule. Redefining shellfish to include cephalopods may expand the exemption under §14.21 and reduce the number of cephalopod shipments subject to this oversight mechanism. The Service should evaluate whether this change could affect its ability to monitor wildlife commerce effectively.

1. Biological inaccuracy Octopuses, squid, and cuttlefish are Cephalopoda, a class of mollusks that lack external shells. Classifying them as shellfish misrepresents their taxonomy and ignores established science. 2. Animal welfare Cephalopods are highly intelligent, capable of learning, problem-solving, and complex behavior. Treating them as shellfish fails...

The term “shellfish” has a well-established meaning in both scientific and common usage. It typically refers to aquatic invertebrates with shells, such as clams, oysters, mussels, and crustaceans like shrimp and crab. Cephalopods are biologically distinct from these organisms. Although they are mollusks, most cephalopods lack an external shell and possess ve...

I appreciate the Service’s effort to update and clarify terminology within the Code of Federal Regulations, and I support the broader goal of aligning regulatory definitions with taxonomic groupings. However, I do not support the inclusion of cephalopods—such as squid, cuttlefish, octopuses, and related taxa—within the definition of “shellfish." While cephal...

The current conditions on shellfish handling maintain quality above the world norm and adding the label shellfish to cephalopods does nothing to bolster this. comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: This is more scientifically correct.

The terminology may be scientifically confusing, but this sounds like an attempt to get out of regulating an industry that harvests octopuses, squid, and other cephalopods for human consumption. Is that in the best interest of managing wild stocks or protecting human health and safety? I oppose the change. If the taxonomy is confusing simply update the termi...

Removing the phrase "having a shell" is ideal and should be done since it does create confusion to people who then think that lobster and crab are the similar to oyster and clam. I am in favor of that aspect but I DO NOT think you should include cephalopods under the term shellfish since that will create additional confusion. These species are harvested and ...

Redefining this when it is known to your agency that these animals are harvested across the globe at a massive scale for human and animal consumption. How do you propose being able to track this in the future with these changes? Do we know longer care about the legality of their harvest?
Comment from California Sea Urchin Commission

comments_clusters.representative_excerpt: This will confuse consumers and is a bad idea. The term shellfish is already confusing to consumers and this will only add to that.

The use of taxonomy for definitions is a good idea. Have you considered the inclusion of the sea cucumbers (Holothuroidea) to cover another group that is consumed - particularly in some Asian cultures.

I oppose this change. Cephalopods should be a separate category. I currently work in the shellfish aquaculture industry and work very hard to promote sustainably raised SHELLFISH as a food option in my community. Educating the public to the benefits of SHELLFISH aquaculture, above every other form of aquaculture, is of great importance to me and should be to...
